elements.sent-2411-001: Data pruning is the problem of identifying a core subset that is most beneficial to training and discarding the remainder.
elements.sent-2411-002: While pruning strategies are well studied for discriminative models like those used in classification, little research has gone into their application to generative models.
elements.sent-2411-003: Generative models aim to estimate the underlying distribution of the data, so presumably they should benefit from larger datasets.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work we aim to shed light on the accuracy of this statement, specifically answer the question of whether data pruning for generative diffusion models (DMs) could have a positive impact.
elements.sent-2411-005: Contrary to intuition, we show that eliminating redundant or noisy data in large datasets is beneficial particularly when done strategically.
elements.sent-2411-006: We experiment with several pruning methods including recent-state-of-art methods, and evaluate over CelebA-HQ and ImageNet datasets.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate that a simple clustering method outperforms other sophisticated and computationally demanding methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: We further exhibit how we can leverage clustering to balance skewed datasets in an unsupervised manner to allow fair sampling for underrepresented populations in the data distribution, which is a crucial problem in generative models.
elements.sent-2411-009: Generative learning has seen tremendous advancement over the past decade, enabling solutions to long-standing challenging problems in image processing such as denoising and upsampling \cite{kawar2022denoising,li2023diffusion,moser2024diffusion , and finding numerous other applications in various domains including speech, image and video generation\cite{richard2021neural, rombach2021highresolution, ho2022imagen, kondratyuk2312videopoet , medical image reconstruction \cite{song2021solving,zhao2023energy,zhang2023generalized,webber2024diffusion and molecule generation \cite{xu2022geodiff,morehead2024geometry .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This remarkable progress is attributed to sophisticated modeling techniques in deep learning such as Variational autoencoders (VAEs) \cite{kingma2013auto , Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)\cite{goodfellow2014generative,gulrajani2017improved,mehralian2018rdcgan , and more recentaly autoregessive transformer models \cite{kondratyuk2312videopoet,sun2024autoregressive and diffusion-based models \cite{song2019generative,ho2020denoising, song2020score,dhariwal2021diffusion .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: These models rely on the availability of extensive large-scale datasets to achieve high-quality outputs, making them highly demanding in terms of computation power, storage and energy.
elements.sent-2411-012: Consequently, this can often limit their training to only powerful machines with substantial resources, rendering them less widely accessible.
elements.sent-2411-013: Existing research predominantly focuses on model pruning \cite{frantar2023sparsegpt,tao2023structured,santacroce2023matters,sun2023simple , where the aim is to optimize the model's parameters by finding the less critical components and discarding them.}
elements.sent-2411-014: These approaches seek to enhance the model efficiency and performance in terms of computational load and memory.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, these efforts concentrate on refining the internal structure of the model rather than addressing data efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, we attempt to alleviate resource consumption by shifting the focus towards pruning of the data, through which we aim to identify redundant or harmful samples and retain a core subset that is sufficiently representative, allowing the model learn more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-017: Scaling behavior refering to how a given performance metric changes in response to change in the dataset size has been studied in machine translation, for example, in the works \cite{gordon2021data,kaplan2020scaling , the authors observe that the cross-entropy loss of supervised machine translation using an autoregressive language model follows a power law, indicating that achieving any improvement on the test error requires exponentially more data.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In large language models based on autoregressive transformers, the work of \cite{hoffmann2022training examines the scaling relationship between a model's size and the amount of training data.}
elements.sent-2411-019: It is proposed that both the data and model should be scaled equally to achieve optimality given a compute budget.
elements.sent-2411-020: In contrast, our research is tailored to examining a generative DM behaviour as we reduce the dataset size while keeping the model fixed.
elements.sent-2411-021: Several works have focused on devising pruning methods for discriminative models, including empirical and more theoretically established methods that provide rigorous theoretical guarantees on the error bound \cite{toneva2018empirical,hwang2020data, coleman2019selection,paul2021deep,mirzasoleiman2020coresets, tan2024data .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Unlike discriminative models which learn decision boundaries to separate the different class categories, generative models aim to model the underlying samples distribution.
elements.sent-2411-023: This implies that the benefit obtained when learning a decision boundary does not necessarily transfer to a distribution modeling task, as the same pruning strategy does not necessarily focus on covering all modes of the distribution, which is essential for training a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-024: This can be explained by the distinct complexity of the loss landscape of generative models in comparison, since they must capture and reproduce intricate patterns within high-dimensional data while using indirect supervision.
elements.sent-2411-025: This higher complexity makes it challenging to understand and infer how different samples contribute during the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, while it might be beneficial to prune samples that minimally affect the empirical risk in discriminative models \cite{tan2024data or samples with a large gradient, it does not necessarily mean that those same samples cannot contribute in learning a distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Consequently, when performing data pruning for generative models, distinct considerations must be given to ensure that the reduced dataset still adequately represents the entire data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-028: It may seem that data pruning harms the performance of a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, assume that we have lots of noisy or redundant data, if we could selectively train using on the high-quality diverse samples, the model will potentially learn to generate more accurate outputs.
elements.sent-2411-030: For instance, let's imagine a large-scale automotive dataset, such datasets illustrate the problem of long-tailed distributions, since much of the data collected on the highway is repetitive or uninteresting, but oftentimes we are interested in situations that capture critical scenarios such as a car overtaking another without maintaining a safe distance.
elements.sent-2411-031: Training generative models on such uncurated datasets is therefore not only resource-inefficient, but also detrimental, as critical driving scenarios are not well represented.
elements.sent-2411-032: Furthermore, generative models trained on unbalanced datasets pose ethical concerns in society when certain groups are underrepresented, further perpetuating existing inequities in society \cite{king2022harmful,teo2023fair,teo2024measuring,hao2023safety,cho2023dall,lee2024holistic,masrourisaadat2024analyzing,zhang2018unreasonable,farina2024ethical .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Data pruning could offer a solution by allowing to control the distribution, guaranteeing balanced distributions so that under-represented populations will be generated equally reliably.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this work, we wish to answer how a redundant or harmful sample can be distinguished, what pruning threshold will break the DM model and how skewed distributions can be balanced.
elements.sent-2411-035: We will see that diffusion models have a high tolerance threshold for pruning.
elements.sent-2411-036: In fact, on ImageNet, we find that we can prune as much as 90\% of the data without any decline in performance.
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, when using clustering in the embedding space of a large visual model, we achieve better performance than when not pruning.
elements.sent-2411-038: These results further imply that even in cases when little data is available, we can still train a DM and obtain reasonable results.
elements.sent-2411-039: Sorting samples by their importance allows to train a discriminative model with a reduced set size while maintaining or minimally affecting their performance, thus saving compute and storage.
elements.sent-2411-040: There exists a body of work that focuses on this problem\cite{har2005smaller, toneva2018empirical,hwang2020data, coleman2019selection,paul2021deep,mirzasoleiman2020coresets, tan2024data,abbas2024effective,xia2022moderate,killamsetty2021grad,kaushal2021prism,zheng2022coverage,yang2022dataset,feldman2020neural,yang2024mind,he2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-041: For example, Toneva \etal \cite{toneva2018empirical assesses a sample's difficulty by tracking the frequency at which the model flips a correct prediction to an incorrect one.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Their empirical findings suggested that samples that are more often forgotten have the most impact on the model.
elements.sent-2411-043: Paul \etal \cite{paul2021deep propose to score samples importance based on the error L2-Norm (EL2N) and the gradient norm (GraNd) early in the training.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Feldman \etal \cite{feldman2020neural score a sample based on the model's ability to memorize and predict its represented subpopulation.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Tan \etal \cite{tan2024data tries to resolve the drawbacks of existing methods such as noisy samples erroneously obtaining high scores despite being harmful.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Instead, they propose Moving-one-Sample-out (MoSo) to score samples based on importance rather than difficulty, by estimating the effect of removing a sample on the empirical risk, or alternatively, scoring high a sample whose gradient matches the average gradient of the data.
elements.sent-2411-047: Works such as \cite{sorscher2022beyond,abbas2024effective,xia2022moderate cluster samples based on their embeddings in a feature space extracted by a strong visual model, and score samples by defining a difficulty metric with respect to the clusters such as distance to the center or region density.}
elements.sent-2411-048: He \etal \cite{he2024large use prediction uncertainty as an indicator for pruning, which measures predictions variation within a sliding window.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Yang \etal \cite{yang2024mind prune samples based on their proximity to the decision boundary, observing that the boundary is most impacted by adjacent samples, as such, selecting those samples ensures that the decision boundary can be reconstructed using a reduced dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The task of generative modeling centers on developing a sampler for an unknown data distribution $p^*(x)$, using independently and identically distributed samples drawn from this distribution.
elements.sent-2411-051: Such a sampler should generate novel samples that follow the same underlying distribution.
elements.sent-2411-052: Within this framework, we introduce the concept of data pruning for generative models, in which given a dataset S used to train a diffusion generative model, we find an optimal training subset $S'\subset S$, that preserves or potentially improves its performance.
elements.sent-2411-053: We experiment with various pruning methods and thoroughly evaluate their effect on the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-054: Diffusion models are a class of generative models that transform simple, noisy data distributions that are easy to sample from into a more complex target distribution $p^*$.
elements.sent-2411-055: By iteratively applying learned transformations, the model reduces the various noise levels added in the forward process until ultimately converging to a real image.
elements.sent-2411-056: The objective during this reverse process is to predict the additive noise through score matching, which guides the mapping to a clean sample.
elements.sent-2411-057: In the following, we expound on the different pruning strategies that we implemented to train a DM model.
elements.sent-2411-058: Below we explain in detail what pruning methods we applied to select a subset of the training data to train a generative DM.
elements.sent-2411-059: For each method, we also apply its inverse, that is, if for a certain method we select samples with the highest scores based on this method's criteria, the inverse is applied on the inverted scores.
elements.sent-2411-060: This helps to gain more insight into the method's efficacy, for example, if its inverse achieves the opposite effect and degenerate the quality, this would indicate that the selection criteria is ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-061: When pruning 50\% based on a method and pruning 50\% based on its inverse, this would create two disjoint training subsets.
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, to check whether something in the middle would yield a more balanced distribution, we takes samples in the middle of these two extremes.
elements.sent-2411-063: These experiments comprise our ablative studies in evaluating each pruning method's effect.
elements.sent-2411-064: To learn how the model behaves as we vary pruning ration, we run each of these pruning methods using several pruning ratios.. \myparagraph{Random pruning .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Samples are randomly pruned without being assessed.
elements.sent-2411-066: Before starting the training, we sample a random subset and use it to train the model throughout the process.
elements.sent-2411-067: This serves as a baseline against which pruning strategies that require computation are evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-068: If a method performs on par with random pruning, it indicates that the strategy might not be as effective in identifying and retaining the most informative samples for training.
elements.sent-2411-069: As the samples are selected uniformly at random, we expect this simple baseline to provide good sample coverage.. \myparagraph{Loss Monotonicity .}
elements.sent-2411-070: This pruning method is inspired by \cite{toneva2018empirical , where the concept of a sample hardness is based on how many times the network flips its correct classification of the sample during training.}
elements.sent-2411-071: This measure can be indicative of the model's uncertainty and difficulty in learning a specific sample.
elements.sent-2411-072: To make this method suitable for DMs, we adapt it based on the monotoncity of the DM objective, which involves minimizing the mean square error (MSE) between the additive noise and predicted noise.
elements.sent-2411-073: As the model learns, it is expected that the general behaviour of this objective is monotonically decreasing.
elements.sent-2411-074: For evaluation fairness of a sample across the different epochs in this phase, we fix the time step $t$ in the diffusion process to a small value, so it is representative of the true distribution rather than the perturbed one.
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, for each sample during training, we keep track of the frequency at which the loss value increases over its previous value for $t$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Samples exhibiting the highest number of increases are selected.
elements.sent-2411-077: The inverse of this strategy selects samples exhibiting the fewest increases.
elements.sent-2411-078: This method requires a short pretraining phase consisting of a small number of epochs to gather data on the loss monotonicity behavior.. \myparagraph{GraNd.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Analogous to \cite{paul2021deep , in which samples difficulty is based on the gradient norm.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Specifically, the method calculates the gradient of the objective with respect to the model's parameters for each sample.
elements.sent-2411-081: In a classification model, this corresponds to the entropy loss, where samples with a larger gradient contribute more to the parameters updates during training.
elements.sent-2411-082: In our context, the score function $\nabla_x \log p_{\theta (x)$, which is estimated by the gradient with respect to the network parameters $\theta$, describes how the likelihood changes with respect to a sample $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Samples in regions with higher density will typically have larger gradients, so retaining these samples should be beneficial for estimating the probability density.
elements.sent-2411-084: This evaluation requires a pretraining phase as well, where in the final epoch we compute the gradient for a fixed small time step $t$ for fair evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-085: We also apply the inverse criteria, which indicates samples in less dense regions are selected.. \myparagraph{EL2N .}
elements.sent-2411-086: In \cite{paul2021deep , sample importance is based on the $\ell_2$ loss magnitude.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The measure scores more high samples it finds challenging as the loss value is indicative of how well the model is performing on each sample.
elements.sent-2411-088: In a DM context, selecting samples with a high loss has the opposite connotation in terms of contribution, as higher loss indicates lower probability density, synonymous to inverse GraNd-based pruning.
elements.sent-2411-089: It might therefore be more beneficial to score samples inversely.
elements.sent-2411-090: This also requires a short pretraining phase where in the final epoch we record the loss for each sample and select those with the highest (lowest) loss value.. \myparagraph{Moving-one-Sample-out (MoSo) .}
elements.sent-2411-091: A recent pruning method that was proposed by Tan \etal \cite{tan2024data .}
elements.sent-2411-092: In this approach, a sample's importance is determined by its impact on the optimal empirical risk.
elements.sent-2411-093: This impact is measured by the change a sample removal from the training set incurs on the empirical risk, which would require retraining a model without the sample in question.
elements.sent-2411-094: Evidently, this is prohibitively expensive, so the authors propose to evaluate the change using first-order approximation, in which they evaluate the extent to which a sample's gradient agrees with those of other samples by measuring its similarity with the average gradient.
elements.sent-2411-095: This method is more robust to outliers, since samples with high scores according to GraNd could merely be indicative of noise rather than valuable information.
elements.sent-2411-096: In a DM context, like for GraNd, it constantly asserts samples are in higher density regions.
elements.sent-2411-097: This method also requires a pretraining phase in which several surrogate networks are trained on multiple subsets of the data and are later used to approximate the similarity using a dot product.
elements.sent-2411-098: This method is the most computationally expensive amongst the methods that we implemented, so we reserve it only to the smaller-scale dataset in our experiments.. \myparagraph{Clustering .}
elements.sent-2411-099: We use a pretrained powerful backbone such as CLIP \cite{radford2021learning and DINO \cite{oquab2023dinov2 to extract the samples features.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: These models yield embeddings containing rich semantic information about the samples, which we use to cluster the samples, ordering them in groups that share similar semantic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-101: For each cluster, we score its population based on their distance from the cluster center, by taking either the samples located closest to its center, the furthest, or those in-between.
elements.sent-2411-102: Samples that are located near the center hold typical features that are representative of the cluster's population distribution.
elements.sent-2411-103: When aggregating all clusters' nearest samples into a training subset, we effectively have representative features across the spectrum, helping retrain the core characteristics of the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-104: When we select samples located furthest away from the center, we cover more difficult and scarce samples, which exposes the model to unique samples.
elements.sent-2411-105: The number of samples selected from each cluster here is proportional to its size, which means that if the distribution of the original data is imbalanced and certain classes are under-representated, the pruned dataset will be equally imbalanced.
elements.sent-2411-106: However, leveraging these clusters, we can easily balance skewed datasets by simply taking an equal number of samples from each cluster.

elements.sent-2411-165: On ImageNet, we observe variation in the behaviour patterns compared to CelebA-HQ.
elements.sent-2411-166: In figure~\ref{fig:fid_imagenet , we notice for example that nearly all pruning strategies were beneficial for training.}
elements.sent-2411-167: In fact, we see that the curves are monotonically decreasing as we increase the pruning ratio with some degradation for GraNd and EL2N$^{-1}$ for $PR>0.5$ and a drastic degrdation when applying Cluster$_{C/D ^{-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Across all PRs, Cluster$_{C/D}$ yields the lowest FID and using DINO features outperforms CLIP features except for $PR>0.75$ where Cluster$_{D}$'s performance drops.
elements.sent-2411-169: At $PR=0.9$ the gap between unpruned and Cluster$_C$ is $\approx 5$ in favor of Cluster$_C$.
elements.sent-2411-170: Another clear different pattern is that GraNd$^{-1}$ performs better than GraNd, as opposed to CelebA-HQ, and EL2N performs worse than EL2N$^{-1}$, possibly indicating the presence of outliers.
elements.sent-2411-171: Also most pruning strategies including their inverse outperform the random baseline, indicating redundancy in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-172: In figure~\ref{fig:fscore_imagenet where we evaluate the F-score, we see that clustering curves behaves in a consistent manner to their FID curves.}
elements.sent-2411-173: EL2N's F-score curve behaves inversely to its FID curve for $PR>0.5$ where the decrease results from a decrease in precision (fidelity).
elements.sent-2411-174: We attach the separate precision and recall curves in the supplementary.
elements.sent-2411-175: In figure~\ref{fig:inception_imagenet , the curves behave in a similar manner to the FID curves with slight fluctuations.}
elements.sent-2411-176: In figure~\ref{fig:vendi_imagenet , cluster$_{D}$'s Vendi score drops more markedly than its FID at $PR>0.75$, indicating that despite the sample high fidelity (as evident from the FID and inception scores), the diversity is noticeably degraded.}
elements.sent-2411-177: We don't provide the average minimum distance for ImageNet as comparing the validation set and generated sets against 1 million samples to find their match is implausible.
elements.sent-2411-178: We observed the sample memorization phenonmen at very high pruning ratios such as $PR=0.99$ when we inspected a few samples.. \myparagraph{Qualitative evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-179: In figure \ref{fig:qual_imagenet we visualise results for different pruning strategies and PRs.}
elements.sent-2411-180: In accordance with the metric evaluation, we can distinguish that Cluster$_{C/D}$ achieves the best perceived quality, in accordance with the quantitative metrics.
elements.sent-2411-181: We include further qualitative results that showcase how the generated samples changes across the different methods for an identical noise vector in the supplementary.
elements.sent-2411-182: Like in CelebA-HQ, on ImageNet we also observe that the generated samples using different pruning strategies converge to similarly looking samples.
elements.sent-2411-183: Figure~\ref{fig:clip_vs_dino compares Cluster$_{C}$ versus Cluster$_{D}$ and Cluster$_{C/D ^{mid}$ when PR=0.5.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: We see that the perceived quality is better when using Cluster$_{D}$, where the samples selected are in closer proximity to the center.

elements.sent-2411-215: In this work we introduced data pruning to generative diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-216: We have conducted a thorough experimental evaluation of the impact different pruning strategies have on the model.
elements.sent-2411-217: Through this evaluation, we showed that data pruning is beneficial for training diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our findings highlight the tolerant nature of DMs to data pruning, demonstrating that unlike other generative models, DMs can maintain high-quality outputs even with significantly reduced training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-219: This makes it possible to utilize DMs even when data is scarce.
elements.sent-2411-220: Preserving the performance when pruning futher makes it possible to steer the pruning in a way that balances the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-221: This study also emphasizes the potential for optimizing training efficiency and resource utilization in generative DMs without compromising their performance.

elements.sent-2411-225: Generative learning has seen tremendous advancement over the past decade, enabling solutions to long-standing challenging problems in image processing such as denoising and upsampling \cite{kawar2022denoising,li2023diffusion,moser2024diffusion , and found numerous other applications in various domains such as speech, image and video generation\cite{richard2021neural, rombach2021highresolution, ho2022imagen, kondratyuk2312videopoet , medical image reconstruction and \cite{song2021solving,zhao2023energy,zhang2023generalized,webber2024diffusion and molecule generation \cite{xu2022geodiff,morehead2024geometry .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Consequently, this can often limit their training to only powerful machines with substantial resources therefore rendering them less widely accessible.
elements.sent-2411-227: Existing research predominantly focuses on model pruning \cite{frantar2023sparsegpt,tao2023structured,santacroce2023matters,sun2023simple , where the aim is to optimize the generative model's parameters by finding the less critical components and discarding them.}
elements.sent-2411-228: In contrast, our attempt to alleviate resource consumption when training generative models shifts the focus towards pruning of the data, through which we aim to identify redundant or harmful samples and retain a core subset that remains sufficiently representative, allowing the model learn most effectively.
elements.sent-2411-229: In contrast, our research is tailored to generative DMs and tries to identify which samples to keep for effective training of these models while keep the model size fixed.
elements.sent-2411-230: Several works have focused on devising pruning methods for discriminative models, including empirical and more theoretically established mthods that provide rigorous theoretical guarantees on the error bound \cite{toneva2018empirical,hwang2020data, coleman2019selection,paul2021deep,mirzasoleiman2020coresets, tan2024data .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Unlike discriminative models which learn decision boundaries to separate the different class categories, generative models aim to model the underlying distribution of the samples.
elements.sent-2411-232: This implies that the benefit obtained by using pruning strategies for learning a decision boundary do not necessarily transfer to a distribution modeling task, as they does not necessarily focus on covering all modes of the distribution, which is essential for training a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-233: It may therefore seem that data pruning would harm the performance of a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-234: For instance, let's imagine a large-scale automotive dataset, such datasets illustare the problem of long-tailed distributions, since much of the data that is collected on the highway is repetitive or uninteresting, but oftentimes we are interested in situations that capture critical scenarios such as a car overtaking another without maintaining a safe distance.
elements.sent-2411-235: Training generative models on such uncurated datasets is therefore not only resource-inefficient, but also detrimental as critical driving scenarios might not be generated in a realistic way.
elements.sent-2411-236: Furthermore, generative models trained on unbalanced datasets pose ethical concerns in society when certain groups are underrepresented, further perpetuating existing inequities \cite{king2022harmful,teo2023fair,teo2024measuring,hao2023safety,cho2023dall,lee2024holistic,masrourisaadat2024analyzing,zhang2018unreasonable,farina2024ethical .}

elements.sent-2411-321: In CelebA-HQ, although visually we observed that the generated samples are more balanced with respect to the underrepresented data, since we are computing the distance with respect to a skewed distribution it is reasonable that the FID becomes lower.
elements.sent-2411-322: The balance can be observed in~\ref{fig:bar_balanced_gen_cluster where the features of the generated samples forms more balanced groups as opposed to pruning based on proportional clustering~\ref{fig:bar_prop_gen_cluster , which more aligns with the original distribution displayed in ~\ref{fig:bar_train_cluster .}}}
elements.sent-2411-323: In ImageNet, however, where the model was much more tolerant towards pruning especially with clustering-based pruning, the FID nearly remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-324: When training using balanced clusters, the model strives to increase the represetations of all samples, as can been seen in figure~\ref{fig:proportional_balanced when compared to proportional clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-325: In this work we introducted data pruning to generative diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-326: Through a comprehensive experimental evaluation, we showed that data pruning is beneficial for training diffusion mdeosl.
